narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path, and we appreciate the unique perspective you bring from your upper-middle-class family background and your choice to attend full-time. The admissions model evaluated your profile and predicted a 68% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which unfortunately led to the rejection of your application—it's a tough threshold designed to prioritize students likely to succeed right away. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from school, especially your Year 3 performance, which ranked in the lowest decile compared to the typical student's mid-range standing and strongly pushed the model toward forecasting a bar failure. Your Year 1 decile, also at the bottom compared to averages around the middle of the pack, was the next biggest factor pulling in the same concerning direction. On a brighter note, your family income level worked in your favor, nudging the prediction slightly toward success since upper-middle-class backgrounds often correlate with stronger outcomes in the data. Similarly, your LSAT score of 32, though below the average of about 37, still offered some positive influence toward passing, as did your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, which is a bit under the typical 3.21 but helped balance things out a little. Being full-time, like most applicants, had almost no impact either way. Overall, your early academic struggles in school stood out as the most influential red flags when compared to students who typically rank higher in their deciles and pass the bar more reliably. This doesn't define your potential—many overcome similar starts through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergrad record with post-bac courses if possible, or seeking programs that value holistic reviews beyond predictive models. We're rooting for you to reapply stronger, and resources like bar prep mentors or academic coaching could make all the difference in rewriting this story. Keep pushing; your voice is needed in law.
